about_page_settings.hero_subtitle: Guiding unforgettable journeys across Africa’s wild landscapes.
about_page_settings.intro_heading: East Africa’s Trusted Safari and Adventure Experts
about_page_settings.intro_paragraph_1: Highlanders Nature Trails is a premier safari and adventure company dedicated to creating unforgettable journeys across East Africa. We specialize in wildlife safaris, mountain expeditions, cultural tours, and personalized travel experiences designed to connect travelers with nature and local communities.
about_page_settings.intro_paragraph_2: Our team combines deep local knowledge with professional service to ensure every journey is safe, comfortable, and memorable.
about_page_settings.fleet_body: We maintain a modern fleet of well-equipped safari vehicles designed for comfort and safety. Our equipment is regularly serviced and inspected to ensure reliability in all terrain and weather conditions.
about_page_settings.team_body: Our experienced team includes professional guides, drivers, porters, chefs, and coordinators who are passionate about delivering exceptional travel experiences.
about_page_settings.safety_body: We prioritize the safety and well-being of our guests. Our operations comply with tourism regulations and international safety standards.
about_page_settings.cta_body: Let our experienced team design a safari or mountain journey tailored just for you.

articles.title: First Light on the Mara
articles.excerpt: Notes from an early morning balloon over the grasslands—and what silence sounds like when the savanna is still waking.
articles.body:
The basket sways once—just enough to remind you that you are suspended over a landscape older than memory. Then the burners hush, and the Mara opens below in shades of umber and sage. This is the hour when night still clings to the long grass but the sky has already made its decision: today will be clear.

Sound before shape

From a vehicle, you learn the reserve in bursts: engine note, radio crackle, tyres on rutted tracks. From a balloon, the first vocabulary is quiet. You hear wildebeest murmuring in loose groups, a fish eagle’s distant complaint, the soft friction of wind over the canopy. Binoculars become less about magnification and more about choosing where to place your attention.

Our guides often say that dawn is when predators finish business started in the dark—and when herbivores negotiate space with posture rather than speed. Watching that negotiation from above is humbling. You are not part of the food web in that moment; you are a witness. The responsibility in that word is something we take seriously on every itinerary we design.

Why height changes empathy

Elevation strips away the temptation to chase sightings. You cannot “turn left” for a closer lion look; you learn to accept the frame the wind gives you. Paradoxically, that constraint deepens appreciation. You begin to notice drainage lines, salt pans, the way fire history writes itself in scrub mosaic. It is a geography lesson with your heart rate slightly elevated—not from fear, but from wonder.

When we land and the champagne toast feels earned, guests often ask whether the balloon is “worth” the early wake-up. We answer with a question: when else will you hear the Mara before you see it? If the answer matters to you, the wake-up is not a cost; it is the price of a rare alignment between body, light, and landscape.

“Silence is not the absence of noise. It is the presence of enough space to listen.”

We would love to weave a balloon morning into your Mara chapter—paired with ground days that balance spectacle with rest. The sky is not the whole story; it is the prologue.
articles.meta_title: First Light on the Mara | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: Travel notes from the Masai Mara at dawn—balloon safari field stories from Highlanders Nature Trails.

articles.title: The Serengeti Migration: Timing Your Visit
articles.excerpt: Rain, grass, and instinct drive one of Earth’s great movements. Here is how seasons shape where the herds are—and how we plan around them.
articles.body:
The Great Migration is often described as a single event with a calendar-friendly address. In truth, it is a rolling negotiation between rainfall, grass chemistry, and the inherited memory of herds that have crossed these plains for millennia. Our job is not to promise a river crossing on a Tuesday, but to place you where probability and comfort align.

Green season vs dry season

After the short rains, the southern plains flush with protein-rich grasses. Wildebeest calving concentrates predators and drama in a relatively compact theatre. Later, as moisture retreats, columns push north and west, seeking biomass that can sustain hundreds of thousands of mouths. The “dry” months sharpen visibility—dust, golden light, and exposed waterholes—but also raise stakes around water access.

We build shoulder-season buffers into itineraries: an extra night near a crossing point, a flexible flight window, a lodge that does not lock you into a single gate. Migration travel rewards humility; the herds do not consult brochures.

River crossings: spectacle and ethics

When animals mass at a bank, tension pulls tight as a drum skin. Crocodiles read the water differently than ungulates do; tourists read cameras differently than guides do. We brief guests on patience—crossings cannot be summoned—and on kindness to other vehicles. A respectful queue preserves sightlines and reduces stress on animals already making life-or-death decisions.

If your dream is drama, we will chase ethical proximity to probability. If your dream is solitude, we may steer you to peripheral corridors where zebra and gazelle stage quieter movements. Either way, the Serengeti rewards those who release the fantasy of control and stay curious about what the day actually offers.

Planning takeaways

  • Book windows, not single dates—especially around crossings.
  • Mix central Serengeti nights with northern or western nodes depending on month.
  • Trust your guide’s morning plan; they synthesize radio intel, weather, and animal behaviour.

Ask us for a month-by-month conversation. Maps are static; rain is not.
articles.meta_title: Serengeti Migration Timing | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: When to visit the Serengeti for the Great Migration—seasonal patterns, river crossings, and itinerary tips.

articles.title: Walking Safaris: Why Pace Changes Everything
articles.excerpt: Without an engine between you and the bush, your senses reorder. Wind, spoor, and bird calls become the map.
articles.body:
A walking safari is not a slow game drive. It is a different discipline—one where sound carries farther than sight, and where the trail is sometimes an old elephant path braided through thorn scrub. Your guide is not only interpreting tracks; they are managing risk with a calm that comes from repetition and respect.

What changes when you step down

Without a chassis around you, wind direction matters again. You learn to pause when baboons cough alarm, to read the posture of giraffe—apparently still but wired for information. Time stretches. A hundred metres can take twenty minutes if the guide is teaching you to differentiate between black and white rhino browse, or to notice the tiny engineering of termite mounds.

Guests often describe walking days as “more tiring yet more restful” than driving days. The paradox makes sense: your body works, but your attention settles. There is no glass between you and the scent of sun-warmed dust after rain.

Safety and trust

We never walk casually where big cats are known to be on a kill, and we never treat proximity as a contest. Weapons and protocols exist, but the core tool is judgement—learned through seasons of reading animal tolerance. Your role is to follow briefings, cluster when asked, and silence phones. The reward is legitimacy: you are a temporary participant in a landscape, not a spectator behind glass.

Who it suits

Reasonable fitness helps—heat and terrain add up—but attitude matters more than marathon times. Curious, patient travellers who can savour small revelations will love walking. If your priority is ticking off a checklist at speed, keep the vehicle days and add one walk as a palate cleanser.

We can blend walking camps with classic driving circuits so you never have to choose a single mode. The bush has room for both; your nervous system will tell you which day felt more honest.
articles.meta_title: Walking Safaris in East Africa | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: What to expect on a guided walking safari—safety, silence, and a deeper connection to the landscape.

articles.title: Okavango Waters: When the Delta Fills
articles.excerpt: Flood pulses from distant highlands rewrite channels and islands each year. Boats, mokoro, and the rhythm of water shape every day.
articles.body:
The Okavango is a miracle of hydrology: water that should drain to the sea pools inland instead, fanning into channels, lagoons, and islands that shift like slow thoughts. That means “best time to visit” is a conversation about water level, not just temperature.

Flood pulse and local rain

Remote Angolan rainfall eventually arrives in Botswana as a rising tide through the panhandle—often months later. Local thunderstorms complicate the picture: a storm near camp can make mokoro trips magical one afternoon and impractical the next. Flexibility is not a buzzword here; it is infrastructure.

We favour camps with multiple activity types—boat, vehicle, mokoro—so guides can pivot without deflating your trip. When water is high, slip through lilies and watch jacana print the surface. When it is low, islands consolidate game around remaining channels, and leopard sightings can spike along wooded margins.

What to expect on the water

Mokoro polers work with astonishing precision in channels barely wider than the vessel. Hippos redefine the word “obstacle.” You will learn to read a ripple differently—to ask whether it is wind, fish, or something heavier. Evenings often end with firelight and frog chorus, the sky enormous above the reeds.

Itinerary philosophy

Pair the Delta with drier reserves if you want contrast—salt pans, Kalahari edges, or a few nights in a more traditional savanna setting. Water teaches patience; dry country teaches scale. Together they make southern Africa feel complete.

Tell us your month and mobility comfort; we will recommend a water-to-land ratio that feels adventurous without feeling chaotic.
articles.meta_title: Okavango Delta Seasons | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: Understanding Okavango flood timing—water levels, activities, and how we build flexible delta itineraries.

articles.title: Responsible Photography on Game Drives
articles.excerpt: Great images should never cost an animal its peace. Flash, distance, and patience are part of the craft—and the ethics.
articles.body:
Everyone arrives with a camera—phone, mirrorless, or long glass that looks like it belongs on a spacecraft. The goal is universal: bring the wild home without stealing peace from the animals that make the wild real.

Distance is dignity

Long lenses exist so that animals can behave naturally. Crowding a sighting for a tighter frame is not creativity; it is pressure. We coach guests to work with light and moment rather than proximity. Often the best image is the honest one: animals relaxed, ears forward, no stress ripple through the herd.

Flash and night drives

Flash photography on nocturnal mammals can disrupt vision and hunting behaviour. Where red-filter spotlights are permitted, we use them sparingly and never to harass. If you are photographing people—guides, staff, community hosts—ask first. Consent is as important in a village as in a city.

Sharing responsibly

Geotagging can expose den sites or rhino territories to poachers faster than conservation teams can adapt. We encourage generic location tags or delayed posts. Your followers will still feel transported; sensitive species will stay safer.

The human frame

Some of the most powerful safari images include human elements: a tracker’s profile, a guide’s hand signalling silence, laughter around a fire. Credit the people in the frame. Safari is collaboration; photographs should honour that.

We love guests who treat images as souvenirs of attention rather than trophies of intrusion. The bush gives generously when we meet it halfway.
articles.meta_title: Ethical Wildlife Photography | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: Field guidelines for safari photography—respecting wildlife, guides, and other guests while capturing the moment.

articles.title: Packing for a Two-Week East Africa Circuit
articles.excerpt: Layers for cool mornings, dust for dry tracks, and a bag that fits small aircraft. A practical list from years on the road.
articles.body:
Two weeks across East Africa might include cool highland mornings, hot savanna middays, and a coastal breeze if you finish on the Indian Ocean. Your bag should be modular: layers you can peel, neutrals that hide dust, and nothing that embarrasses you at a lodge dinner.

Luggage reality

Many inter-camp flights use small aircraft with strict weight limits—often 15 kg in soft bags. We send a checklist early, but the rule of thumb is: one primary bag, one daypack, optics around your neck, not in the hold. Laundry in camp is normal; overpacking is the enemy.

Clothing

  • Light long-sleeved shirts for sun and insect protection.
  • A warm mid-layer and a packable shell for dawn drives and open vehicles.
  • Wide-brim hat plus a buff for dust on dry tracks.
  • Comfortable closed shoes for walking; sandals for camp.

Optics and tech

Binoculars matter as much as cameras—8x42 is a versatile sweet spot. Bring spare batteries, a small power bank, and adapters suited to lodges you will visit. If you shoot video, prioritize cards and stability; safari days generate more footage than you expect.

Health and comfort

Pack prescriptions in original containers, sunscreen you will actually reapply, lip balm, and moisturizer—aircraft and dust are dehydrating. Consult your clinician about malaria prophylaxis where relevant. A compact personal first-aid kit with blister plasters and antihistamine covers minor annoyances before they become distractions.

Travel light, plan laundry, and leave room for the intangible: the jacket you will loan someone on a chilly morning, or the book you finish by lantern light. Those extras weigh nothing and mean everything.
articles.meta_title: Safari Packing List — Two Weeks | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: What to pack for a multi-stop East Africa safari—clothing, optics, health kit, and luggage limits.

articles.title: Community Conservancies and Where Your Fees Go
articles.excerpt: Bed-night levies and conservancy fees fund rangers, schools, and grazing agreements. Transparency builds trust—and better landscapes.
articles.body:
Community conservancies expand habitat beyond national park boundaries by aligning local livelihoods with wildlife presence. They are not charity projects; they are contracts—often negotiated over years—about land use, grazing rotations, and revenue sharing.

Where the money flows

Bed-night fees and conservancy levies typically fund security patrols, wildlife monitoring, compensation schemes for livestock losses, and social investments like scholarships or clinics. Transparency varies, and we favour partners who publish impact summaries and involve community governance structures in decisions.

When you stay in a conservancy property, you are not “buying a view.” You are paying into a corridor that lets elephants move, lions disperse, and grass recover after drought. That is infrastructure in ecological form.

What guests can do

Ask questions—respectfully—about how your lodge contributes beyond marketing language. Visit community projects when offered; buy crafts directly from artisans. Tip guides and staff in line with camp norms; good wages and tip pools often circle back into local economies.

The long horizon

Conservancies face pressure from climate shifts, development corridors, and political cycles. Tourism alone cannot solve conservation, but aligned tourism buys time for science, policy, and local stewardship to synchronize. Your trip can be both pleasure and participation if you choose partners who treat fees as trust, not just revenue.

We are happy to map conservancy stays into your route—not as an add-on, but as a coherent strategy for impact and exceptional sightings alike.
articles.meta_title: Community Conservancies Explained | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: How safari tourism supports community-owned conservancies in Kenya and Tanzania—fees, impact, and long-term stewardship.

articles.title: Above the Clouds: An Afternoon on Mount Kenya
articles.excerpt: Teleki Valley and giant groundsels feel like another planet. Altitude humbles you; the silence between gusts does the rest.
articles.body:
Mount Kenya is not Kilimanjaro’s taller sibling in the brochure sense; it is a more intricate mountain—glaciers hanging above valleys carved by ice that has mostly retreated, leaving moraines and tarns that glitter like spilled coins. An afternoon above the clouds can feel like trespassing in a garden designed for giants.

Afro-alpine theatre

Giant groundsel and lobelia look imported from science fiction. They are simply evolution’s answer to cold nights and intense UV. Guereza colobus bark somewhere in the podocarp forest below; closer to the treeline, hyrax whistles stitch the silence. Altitude narrows your vocabulary to essentials: water, pace, gratitude.

We do not rush acclimatization. A sluggish headache is information, not failure. Guides who know the mountain translate weather signs—cap clouds, wind shifts—into pace adjustments that keep you safer than any slogan.

Pairing peaks with plains

Many travellers combine a few trekking days with the Mara or Laikipia. The contrast is therapeutic: vertical solitude, then horizontal abundance. Your legs may disagree at first, but your imagination will not. The mountain teaches scale; the savanna teaches flow.

Practical notes

  • Train with hills and stairs; descents punish unprepared quads.
  • Layer obsessively—sun at noon, frost at dawn.
  • Respect park rules and ranger guidance; erosion is already a threat on busy trails.

If you have ever wanted silence with altitude, Mount Kenya delivers—one careful step at a time, above a sea of cloud that makes the lowlands feel like another continent.
articles.meta_title: Mount Kenya Alpine Trek | Highlanders Nature Trails
articles.meta_description: Field notes from the afro-alpine zone on Mount Kenya—acclimatization, flora, and combining peaks with safari.

mountains.name: 2-Day Mount Kenya Trek to Lake Ellis & Nithi Waterfall Itinerary
mountains.description:
2-Day Mount Kenya Trek to Lake Ellis & Nithi Waterfall

Destination: Mount Kenya
Route: Sirimon Gate – Lake Ellis – Nithi Waterfall
Duration: 2 Days / 1 Night
Difficulty Level: Moderate
Best For: Adventure seekers, nature lovers, team building, and weekend getaways
Altitude Range: 2,650m – 3,470m above sea level

Day 1: Nairobi – Sirimon Gate – Trek to Lake Ellis

Departure: Early morning from Nairobi
Drive Time: Approximately 4–5 hours

Your adventure begins with an early morning departure from Nairobi, heading towards the northwestern side of Mount Kenya. Upon arrival at Sirimon Gate, you will complete park registration and meet your experienced guides and porters.

The trek starts through beautiful montane forest filled with unique wildlife, birds, and scenic landscapes. As you ascend, the forest gradually opens into moorland with spectacular views of the mountain peaks.

You will arrive at Lake Ellis in the afternoon, a serene alpine lake surrounded by rolling hills and dramatic mountain scenery.

Activities

  • Guided nature walk
  • Photography and relaxation
  • Optional short evening hike
  • Sunset views over Mount Kenya

Meals Included: Lunch and Dinner
Accommodation: Camping at Lake Ellis Campsite

Day 2: Lake Ellis – Nithi Waterfall – Return to Nairobi

Wake up early to the fresh mountain air and enjoy a hearty breakfast before beginning your morning hike.

You will trek through scenic moorlands and descend toward the beautiful Nithi Waterfall, one of the hidden gems of Mount Kenya. Here, you can relax, take photos, and enjoy the peaceful sounds of cascading water surrounded by lush vegetation.

After spending time at the waterfall, you will continue your descent and transfer back to Nairobi, arriving in the evening.

Activities

  • Morning hike
  • Visit to Nithi Waterfall
  • Nature photography
  • Scenic drive back to Nairobi

Meals Included: Breakfast and Lunch

Package Includes

  • Transport from Nairobi and back
  • Professional mountain guide and support team
  • Park entry fees
  • Camping equipment
  • Meals as per itinerary
  • Bottled drinking water
  • First aid support

Package Excludes

  • Personal hiking gear
  • Travel insurance
  • Tips and gratuities
  • Personal expenses

What to Carry

  • Warm clothing and rain jacket
  • Comfortable hiking boots
  • Water bottle
  • Sunscreen and hat
  • Camera
  • Personal toiletries

mountains.name: 2-Day Mount Kilimanjaro Trek – Marangu Route Adventure
mountains.description:
Destination: Mount Kilimanjaro
Route: Marangu Gate – Mandara Hut
Duration: 2 Days / 1 Night
Difficulty Level: Moderate
Best For: Beginners, weekend hikers, team building, and short mountain adventures
Highest Altitude: 2,700 meters

Trip Overview

Experience the magic of Mount Kilimanjaro, Africa’s highest mountain, on a short but rewarding trekking adventure via the famous Marangu Route. This 2-day trek is perfect for travelers who want to explore the mountain without attempting the full summit climb.

You will hike through lush rainforest, spot unique wildlife, enjoy breathtaking scenery, and spend a night on the mountain before descending the next day.

Day 1: Arusha / Moshi – Marangu Gate – Mandara Hut

Your journey begins with a morning transfer to Marangu Gate, the main entry point to Mount Kilimanjaro National Park.

After registration and briefing, the trek begins through dense rainforest filled with towering trees, monkeys, and colorful birds. The trail is well-maintained and gradually ascends to Mandara Hut, where you will spend the night.

Distance: ~8 km
Hiking Time: 4–5 hours
Habitat: Rainforest

Highlights:

  • Guided mountain trek
  • Wildlife and bird watching
  • Scenic forest landscapes
  • Sunset views on the mountain

Meals: Lunch & Dinner
Accommodation: Mandara Hut

Day 2: Mandara Hut – Marangu Gate – Return

After breakfast, you will take a short optional walk to the Maundi Crater viewpoint, offering panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.

You will then descend back through the rainforest to Marangu Gate, receive your trekking certificate, and transfer back to your hotel in Arusha or Moshi.

Distance: ~8 km
Hiking Time: 3–4 hours
Habitat: Rainforest

Highlights:

  • Maundi Crater viewpoint
  • Scenic descent through forest
  • Certificate presentation
  • Return transfer

Meals: Breakfast & Lunch

Quick Facts

  • Country: Tanzania
  • Mountain Height: 5,895 meters
  • Best Time to Visit: January–March, June–October
  • Group Size: Flexible (private or group)
  • Age Limit: Typically 10 years and above

Package Includes

  • Park entry fees
  • Professional mountain guide
  • Accommodation in mountain huts
  • Meals during the trek
  • Drinking water
  • Transfers to and from the park
  • Rescue fees

Package Excludes

  • International travel
  • Personal hiking gear
  • Travel insurance
  • Tips and gratuities
  • Personal expenses

mountains.name: Mount Kenya Scenic Flight Tour
mountains.description:
Destination: Mount Kenya
Tour Type: Scenic Flight Experience
Duration: Approximately 1 Hour
Departure Point: Nairobi / Nanyuki
Best For: Photography, luxury experiences, special occasions, tourists, and corporate groups

Tour Overview

Discover the breathtaking beauty of Mount Kenya from the sky on an unforgettable scenic flight. This exclusive aerial experience offers panoramic views of Africa’s second-highest mountain, its snow-capped peaks, glaciers, alpine lakes, and dramatic valleys—all within a short and comfortable flight.

Perfect for travelers who want to experience the majesty of the mountain without trekking, the flight provides a unique perspective of Kenya’s most iconic natural landmark.

Flight Experience Highlights

  • Aerial views of the majestic Mount Kenya peaks
  • Fly over glaciers, valleys, and alpine landscapes
  • Stunning photography opportunities
  • Comfortable and safe scenic flight
  • Ideal for proposals, celebrations, and special occasions
  • Suitable for all ages

Sample Flight Itinerary

Departure – Nairobi / Nanyuki Airstrip

Your journey begins with arrival at the departure airstrip, where you will receive a safety briefing from the pilot before boarding the aircraft.

Shortly after takeoff, you will head toward the towering peaks of Mount Kenya, enjoying spectacular views of forests, rivers, and wildlife reserves along the way.

Aerial Tour Around Mount Kenya

During the flight, you will circle the mountain, providing breathtaking views of iconic features such as:

  • Batian Peak (Highest peak)
  • Nelion Peak
  • Glaciers and alpine lakes
  • Moorlands and rugged valleys

Your pilot may also point out wildlife areas and scenic landmarks visible from the air.

Return Flight

After completing the scenic circuit around the mountain, the aircraft will return to the departure airstrip, concluding your unforgettable aerial adventure.

Duration Options

  • 30 Minutes – Quick scenic flight
  • 45 Minutes – Standard flight experience
  • 60 Minutes – Extended scenic tour (recommended)

Package Includes

  • Scenic flight around Mount Kenya
  • Professional pilot and safety briefing
  • Window seat for photography
  • Fuel and aviation fees
  • Passenger insurance

Package Excludes

  • Transport to the airstrip
  • Meals and beverages
  • Personal expenses
  • Optional photography services

Best Time for the Flight

  • Morning: Clear skies and best visibility
  • Dry Seasons: January–March and June–October

site_settings.value: Discover Africa Beyond the Ordinary Safari Experiences Like Never Before
Luxury safaris, mountain adventures, and unforgettable journeys.
Strategic Safari Experiences , Authentic. Seamless. Unforgettable

We transform travel dreams into extraordinary adventures. Since 2014, our team has guided explorers across Africa’s most iconic landscapes, crafting personalized journeys and delivering world-class safari experiences with precision and genuine care.

We create journeys that inspire discovery, build lasting memories, and deliver unforgettable moments in the heart of the wild.
Speak with our travel designers and build an itinerary tailored to you.
Passionate guides, sustainable travel, unforgettable horizons.
We are a team of explorers and conservation advocates dedicated to sharing Africa's wonders responsibly.
© 2026 Highlanders Nature Trails . All rights reserved | Powered By Designekta Studios

At Highlanders Nature Trails, we are committed to protecting your privacy and ensuring that your personal information is handled in a safe and responsible manner. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and safeguard your information when you visit our website or book our tours and travel services.

Information We Collect

We may collect personal information including but not limited to:

  • Full name
  • Email address
  • Phone number
  • Travel preferences and booking details
  • Payment information
  • Emergency contact details (for safety purposes during trips)

We also collect non-personal information such as browser type, device information, and website usage statistics to improve our services and user experience.

How We Use Your Information

Your information may be used to:

  • Process tour bookings and reservations
  • Communicate important travel updates and confirmations
  • Provide customer support
  • Improve our website and services
  • Ensure safety and emergency preparedness during trips
  • Comply with legal and regulatory requirements

We do not sell, rent, or share your personal information with third parties for marketing purposes.

Data Security

We implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect your personal information against unauthorized access, loss, misuse, or alteration.

Third-Party Services

Our website may contain links to third-party websites or services such as payment gateways, booking platforms, or social media channels. We are not responsible for the privacy practices of those external websites.

Cookies

Our website may use cookies to enhance user experience, analyze website traffic, and personalize content. You can choose to disable cookies through your browser settings.

Your Rights

You have the right to:

  • Access your personal information
  • Request correction of inaccurate data
  • Request deletion of your data where applicable
  • Withdraw consent for communications

Updates to This Policy

We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with the updated effective date.

Welcome to Highlanders Nature Trails. By booking a tour or using our website, you agree to the following Terms and Conditions. Please read them carefully.

Booking and Payments

  • Bookings are confirmed upon receipt of the required deposit or full payment.
  • Payment methods may include bank transfer, mobile money, or approved payment platforms.
  • Prices are subject to change without prior notice due to factors such as fuel costs, park fees, or government regulations.

Cancellations and Refunds

  • Cancellations made within the specified period may be eligible for a partial refund.
  • Last-minute cancellations or no-shows may result in forfeiture of payment.
  • Refund timelines may vary depending on the payment method used.

Travel Responsibilities

Participants are responsible for:

  • Providing accurate personal information
  • Ensuring they are physically fit for the selected tour or trek
  • Carrying valid identification and required travel documents
  • Following instructions provided by guides and safety personnel

Safety and Liability

Highlanders Nature Trails prioritizes safety; however, outdoor activities such as hiking, trekking, camping, and wildlife viewing involve inherent risks.

By participating in our tours, you acknowledge that:

  • You voluntarily assume responsibility for personal safety
  • The company is not liable for injuries, delays, or losses caused by circumstances beyond our control, including weather conditions, natural hazards, or third-party actions

Changes to Itineraries

We reserve the right to modify itineraries due to weather conditions, safety concerns, park regulations, or operational requirements.

Governing Law

These Terms and Conditions shall be governed by the laws of the Republic of Kenya.

Highlanders Nature Trails uses photographs and visual content to showcase destinations, wildlife, landscapes, and travel experiences. We respect the intellectual property rights of photographers, partners, and content creators.

Image Sources

Photos displayed on this website may come from:

  • Highlanders Nature Trails team members and guides
  • Professional photographers and content partners
  • Licensed stock photography platforms
  • Tourism boards and conservation organizations
  • Guests and travelers who have granted permission

Rights and Permissions

All images remain the property of their respective owners and are used with permission or appropriate licensing.

If you believe that an image on our website has been used without proper credit or authorization, please contact us immediately so we can address the matter promptly.
